title: Heat can be deadly, but sunshine itself? Science says we could use more of it | Rowan Jacobsen
content: # Heat Can Be Deadly, but Sunshine Itself? Science Says We Could Use More of It **LOPINUZE Senior Editor** — As the UK endures another high-summer heatwave, public health officials are once again urging caution: seek shade, wear sunscreen, and cover up. But according to emerging scientific research reviewed by Rowan Jacobsen, a former Knight Science Journalism Fellow at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and media fellow at the Nova Institute for Health in Baltimore, the prevailing wisdom may be dangerously one-sided. “Those who spend more time in the sun tend to be healthier. A *lot* healthier,” Jacobsen writes in his new book, *In Defense of Sunlight*. The statement challenges decades of dermatological orthodoxy that has painted all sun exposure as inherently risky. The core finding: while extreme exposure to ultraviolet radiation can cause skin cancer, moderate, regular sunlight exposure is associated with a 30-40% reduction in all-cause mortality, according to meta-analyses cited in Jacobsen’s research. The protective effects appear linked to vitamin D production, nitric oxide release—which lowers blood pressure—and circadian rhythm regulation. ## The Data Behind the Sunlight Debate Jacobsen’s investigation, spanning nine years and culminating in his book published this month, reveals a striking paradox. In countries with limited sunshine—including the UK—rates of vitamin D deficiency hover around 20-25% of the population during winter months. Deficiency has been linked to increased risks of cardiovascular disease, certain cancers, and autoimmune disorders. “We’ve gone too far the other way,” Jacobsen told LOPINUZE Science Desk. “The message should be about intelligent exposure, not blanket avoidance. A 10- to 15-minute midday walk in summer, with arms and face uncovered, can produce enough vitamin D for most people without significantly increasing skin cancer risk.” Dr. Emily Hartwell, a dermatologist at University College London who reviewed the research independently, offered a nuanced perspective: “The data is compelling that moderate sun exposure has systemic health benefits. My concern is that people will interpret this as permission to bake. The key is short, non-burning exposure—not sunbathing.” ## Balancing Risk and Reward The American Academy of Dermatology continues to recommend sunscreen for any outdoor activity lasting more than 15 minutes. However, Jacobsen points to epidemiological data showing that office workers who get limited sun have higher rates of several chronic diseases than outdoor workers who receive regular, non-burning exposure. “The science is clear that sun avoidance has its own health costs,” Jacobsen said. “We need a public health message that acknowledges both the risks of overexposure and the risks of underexposure.” ## Forward-Looking Analysis The debate is likely to intensify as climate change pushes summer temperatures higher. UK heatwaves have become 30% more frequent over the past decade, according to Met Office data. Public health campaigns may need to evolve from simple “seek shade” messaging to more nuanced guidance: protect against heatstroke during peak hours (11 a.m. to 3 p.m.) while encouraging brief, unprotected exposure during safer morning or late-afternoon windows. Jacobsen’s book arrives at a moment when the conversation around sunlight is shifting. Researchers at the Nova Institute for Health are now conducting a randomized controlled trial to quantify the benefits of daily sunlight exposure versus supplementation. Early results are expected within 18 months. For now, the message from science is clear: the sun is not our enemy—but neither is it safe to ignore its power. Moderation, as with most things in health, appears to be the wisest course.
